By His saying: وَأَيَّدَكُمْ بِنَصْرِهِ "and strengthened you with His help," He refers to the favor of victory He bestowed upon them at Badr. By His saying: وَرَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ "and provided you with good things," He refers to the spoils of war (الغنائم) that He provided for them and made lawful.

Although the conditions of the believers and His favors upon them regarding shelter mentioned in the verse are specific to the Emigrants (المهاجرين) among them rather than the Helpers (الأنصار), the intent is to bestow favor upon all of them—both Emigrants and Helpers—for they are a single nation (أمة واحدة) united by one religion. Furthermore, the favors Allah mentioned in the verse, such as strengthening with victory and providing from the good things, encompass everyone. This is according to what the verse necessitates by virtue of its occurrence within the context of the verses of Badr. However, taken alone and by itself, it encompasses all Muslims insofar as they are a single nation whose later generations trace back to their predecessors. The manifestation of Islam began among them while they were few and abased in Mecca, fearing that people would snatch them away; then He sheltered them in Medina, increased their numbers through the Helpers, strengthened them with His victory at Badr and elsewhere, and provided them with all good things—spoils and other blessings—so that they might be grateful.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "O you who have faith! Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts knowingly" (8:27) to the end of the two verses. Betrayal (الخيانة) — the violation of a trust (الأمانة), which is the preservation of security for a right among rights through a covenant, a will, or the like. Al-Raghib said: "Betrayal (الخيانة) and hypocrisy (النفاق) are one and the same, except that betrayal is used in consideration of the covenant and trust, while hypocrisy is used in consideration of religion; then they overlap. Thus, betrayal is the opposition to truth by breaking a covenant in secret. The opposite of betrayal is trustworthiness (الأمانة). It is said: 'I betrayed so-and-so' or 'I betrayed the trust of so-and-so.' Based on this is His saying: لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ (Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts)." End quote.

His saying: وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ "and [do not] betray your trusts" may be in the jussive mood (مجزوماً), coordinated with the preceding "do not betray" (لا تخونوا), meaning: "and do not betray your trusts." It may also be in the subjunctive mood (منصوباً) due to the omission of the particle an (أن), the implied meaning being: "and [lest] you betray your trusts." The second view is supported by His subsequent saying: وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "knowingly."

This is because betrayal, although the prohibitory ruling (النهي التحريمي) only attaches to it upon knowledge (العلم)—for there is no prohibition without knowledge of the subject matter, nor any sanctity—knowledge is nonetheless among the general conditions (الشرائط العامة) without which no masterly obligation (تكليف مولوي) is actualized. Thus, there is no apparent subtle point (نكتة) in restricting the prohibition of betrayal to knowledge, since knowledge, being a general condition, does not require explicit mention. The apparent meaning of His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "while you know"—with the object of the verb omitted—is: "while you have knowledge that it is a betrayal." It is not as some have said: "while you know the corruptions of betrayal, its evil consequences, and Allah's prohibition of it," for there is no evidence for that from the perspective of the wording or the context.

The correct view is that the sentence implies: "and [lest] you betray your trusts," and the entirety of His saying: لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ constitutes a single prohibition concerning a specific type of betrayal, which is the betrayal of the trust of Allah and His Messenger, which is itself a betrayal of the trust of the believers themselves. For among trusts, some are the trust of Allah (أمانة الله) with the people, such as His legislated rulings; some are the trust of the Messenger (أمانة الرسول), such as his noble conduct (سيرته الحسنة); and some are the trusts of the people with one another, such as their wealth or their secrets. There are also trusts shared by Allah, His Messenger, and the believers, which are the matters commanded by Allah, implemented by the Messenger, and which benefit the people and form the backbone of their society—such as political secrets and military objectives, the disclosure of which would ruin the hopes of the religion and lead the efforts of the Islamic government astray, thereby nullifying the right of Allah and His Messenger and resulting in harm to the generality of the believers.

This type of trust—its betrayal is a betrayal of Allah, the Messenger, and the believers. The betrayer among the believers who commits this act betrays Allah and the Messenger while knowing that this trust he is betraying is a trust for himself and for the rest of his believing brothers; thus, he is betraying his own trust. No rational person would intentionally betray his own trust, for a human being, through the intellect (العقل) bestowed upon him, perceives the ugliness of betraying a trust—so how could he betray his own trust?

Therefore, the meaning of His saying: وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ —and Allah knows best—is: "and you betray, within the betrayal of Allah and the Messenger, your own trusts, while the case is that you know they are your own trusts yet you betray them." What rational person would proceed to betray his own trust and cause harm that only returns to his own person? Thus, concluding the prohibition with His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "knowingly" is to stir righteous zeal (العصبية الحقة) and to provoke the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة), not to state one of the conditions of religious obligation (التكليف).

It is as if some individuals among the Muslims were disclosing matters of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) hidden determinations from the polytheists or informing them of some of his secrets; so Allah called it betrayal and forbade it, counting it as a betrayal of Allah, the Messenger, and the believers.

This is supported by His saying after this prohibition: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ "Know that your possessions and your children are only a test..." (8:28) etc. The apparent context is that it is connected to what preceded it and not independent of it. It implies then that His exhorting them regarding wealth and children alongside the prohibition of betraying Allah, the Messenger, and their trusts was because the one among them who informed the polytheists of the Messenger of Allah's hidden secrets did so to gain favor with them, fearing they might transgress against his wealth and children whom he had left in Mecca upon migrating to Medina. Thus, they began informing them of news to establish affection and preserve wealth and children, or something similar, akin to what occurred with Abu Lubabah regarding the Banu Qurayzah.

This supports what has been narrated concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول): that Abu Sufyan left Mecca with much wealth, so Gabriel informed the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of his departure and advised him to go out against him and keep the matter secret. However, one of them wrote to him with the news, so Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَماناتِكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ. There are other narrations regarding the revelation of the verse that will come, Allah willing, in the following traditional discussion.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً وَيُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ "O you who have faith! If you are God-conscious, He will grant you a criterion, and absolve you of your misdeeds, and forgive you, and Allah is the Lord of great bounty" (8:29). The Criterion (الفرقان) — that by which one distinguishes between one thing and another. In the verse, by the evidence of the context and its derivation from godwariness (التقوى), it is the criterion between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), whether that be in belief—by distinguishing between faith and disbelief, and every guidance and misguidance—or in action—by distinguishing between obedience and disobedience, and everything that pleases Allah or angers Him—or in opinion and insight—by separating between correctness and error. All of this is what the tree of godwariness produces. The term "Criterion" is used unconditionally in the verse and not restricted, as the previous verses have enumerated various types of good and evil, and all of them require a criterion.

Similar to this verse in meaning is His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَحْتَسِبُ وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ "And whoever is God-conscious, He will make for him a way out, and provide for him from whence he does not reckon. And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him" (65:2-3). The discussion regarding the meaning of the "absolving of misdeeds" and "forgiveness" has already passed. The verse serves as a summary of the discourse regarding the commands and prohibitions contained in the preceding verses; that is: if you are God-conscious, that which pleases Allah in all that has passed will not be confused for you with that which angers Him, and He will absolve you of your misdeeds and forgive you, and Allah is the Lord of great bounty.

(Commentary)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from 'Aqil al-Khuza'i: that the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) said: "Indeed, terror and fear of the struggle (الجهاد) against those deserving of struggle—and those who cooperate in misguidance—is misguidance in religion and a stripping away of the worldly life along with humiliation and belittlement. In it is the deserving of the Fire by fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف) at the presence of combat. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ (O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless on the battlefield, do not turn your backs to them)."

In al-Faqih and al-'Ilal, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Shadhan: that Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) wrote to him among the answers to his questions: "Allah has forbidden fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف) because of the weakness it causes in the religion, the belittling of the Messengers and the just Imams, the abandonment of aiding them against enemies, and the punishment for them for abandoning what they were called to—of acknowledging the Lordship, manifesting justice, abandoning oppression, and putting an end to corruption—due to the resulting boldness of the enemy against the Muslims, and the ensuing captivity, killing, nullification of the religion of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and other forms of corruption."

I say: The narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are exhaustive (مستفيضة) that fleeing from the battlefield is among the major destructive sins (المعاصي الكبيرة الموبقة). A portion of this has already passed in the discussion on major sins in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تَجْنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31) in the fourth volume of this book.

There are also narrations to this effect from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah, as in the two Sahihs of al-Bukhari and Muslim, from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: اجتنبوا السبع الموبقات — "Avoid the seven destructive sins." They asked: "And what are they, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: الشرك بالله وقتل النفس التي حرم الله إلا بالحق والسحر وأكل الربا وأكل مال اليتيم والتولي يوم الزحف وقذف المحصنات الغافلات المؤمنات — "Associating partners with Allah; killing the soul which Allah has forbidden except by right; sorcery; consuming usury; consuming the property of the orphan; turning away on the day of the battlefield; and slandering chaste, oblivious, believing women." There are other narrations from Ibn 'Abbas and others indicating that fleeing from the battlefield is among the major sins.

Yes, His saying, the Exalted: الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً فَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ صابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ "Now Allah has lightened your burden, and He knows that there is a weakness in you. So if there be a hundred of you, patient, they shall overcome two hundred..." (8:66) restricts the absolute nature of the verse prohibiting fleeing to cases where the ratio is less than three to one.

It has been narrated through their paths from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, Ibn 'Abbas, Abu Hurayrah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and others, as in al-Durr al-Manthur: that the prohibition of fleeing from the battlefield in this verse was specific to the day of Badr.

This has sometimes been justified by the claim that the verse was revealed on the day of Badr, and that the adverbial phrase in His saying: وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ "and whoever turns his back to them on that day" (8:16) refers to the day of Badr. However, you have already seen that the context of the verses testifies to their revelation after the day of Badr, and that the intended meaning of "on that day" (يومئذ) is the day of the battlefield (يوم الزحف), not specifically the day of Badr. Furthermore, even if we were to assume its revelation on the day of Badr, the specificity of the occasion (خصوص السبب) does not affect the generality of the verse's significance (عموم المدلول), as is the case with all other verses that combine general significance with a specific occasion.

The author of al-Manar said in his exegesis: "Basing the specification on the circumstantial evidence (قرينة الحال) might only be plausible if the verse had been revealed before the engagement of combat—contrary to the majority view—given the unique characteristics of the Battle of Badr, such as its being the first battle in Islam; had the Muslims been defeated while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them, the tribulation (الفتنة) would have been immense. Also, the strengthening of the Muslims with angels to make them firm, and His promise to grant them victory and cast terror into the hearts of their enemies.

So, if we look at the collective characteristics and the circumstantial evidence in the prohibition, the view that the prohibition coupled with the severe threat in the verse was specific to it becomes plausible. Add to this that Allah, the Exalted, tested the Companions (رض) with turning away and retreating in battle twice while he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was with them: on the day of Uhud, regarding which Allah, the Exalted, says: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا مِنْكُمْ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ إِنَّمَا اسْتَزَلَّهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ بِبَعْضِ ما كَسَبُوا وَلَقَدْ عَفَا اللهُ عَنْهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ 'Those of you who turned back on the day when the two hosts met, it was only Satan who made them slip because of some of what they had earned. But Allah has certainly excused them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing' (3:155); and on the day of Hunayn, regarding which Allah, the Exalted, says: لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ فِي مَواطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'Allah has certainly helped you in many situations, and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number impressed you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse; then you turned back [and] fled. Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the believers...' (9:25-26). This does not negate that turning away is forbidden and among the major sins, nor does it necessitate that every instance of turning away for reasons other than the two exceptions in the verse of al-Anfal causes its perpetrator to incur a great wrath from Allah and have his abode in Hell—an evil destination. Rather, it may be less than that, and it is restricted by the verse of the license for weakness (رخصة الضعف) coming later in this Surah, and by the prohibition of throwing oneself into destruction (التهلكة) in its generality, as previously discussed in Surah al-Baqarah and as will be detailed shortly.

Ahmad and the authors of the Sunan, except al-Nasa'i, narrated from the hadith of Ibn 'Umar who said: 'I was in one of the detachments of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and the people fled a flight, and I was among those who fled—so we said: "What shall we do—having fled from the battlefield and incurred the wrath?" Then we said: "What if we entered Medina and stayed the night?" Then we said: "What if we presented ourselves to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)? If there is repentance for us, [well and good], otherwise we shall depart." So we came to him before the morning prayer—and he came out and said: "Who are the fleers (الفرارون)?" We said: "We are the fleers." He said: "Nay, you are the returners to the fight (العكارون); I am your party (فئتكم) and the party of the Muslims." He said: "So we came until we kissed his hand."'

And the wording of Abu Dawud: 'So we said: "Let us enter Medina and stay the night therein—so we may go and no one sees us." So we entered and said: "What if we presented ourselves to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—if there is repentance for us, we shall stay—and if it is otherwise, we shall depart." So we sat for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before the dawn prayer—and when he came out, we stood up to him and said: "We are the fleers..."' etc.

Some have interpreted this hadith with an expansion in the meaning of 'withdrawing to a party' (التحيز إلى فئة) such that no meaning remains for the threat, nor any rule for the language. Al-Tirmidhi said regarding it: 'It is fair (حسن); we do not know it except from the hadith of Yazid ibn Abi Ziyad.' I say: He is disputed; many have weakened him. Ibn Hibban said: 'He was truthful, except that when he grew old, his memory failed and he changed, so fabrications occurred in his hadith; so whoever heard from him before the change, his hearing is sound.' The summary of the matter is: this hadith has no weight in this issue, neither in its text (متناً) nor its chain (سنداً), and in its meaning is a report from 'Umar which is even less than it, so it is not placed in the scales of this issue." End quote.

I say: That which he cited at the beginning of his speech regarding the reasons and circumstantial evidence surrounding the Battle of Badr—such as its being the first battle in Islam, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being among them, and the like—are shared in terms of the reality of the underlying rationale (الملاك) between it and battles like Uhud, al-Khandaq, Khaybar, and Hunayn. Islam in those days was in dire need of fighting men and their steadfastness in battlefields, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them, and Allah promised them victory and sent down angels in some of them to strengthen them and cast terror into the hearts of their enemies.

As for the verses he mentioned that were revealed concerning their flight on the day of Uhud and the day of Hunayn, there is no indication in them that the threat of the verse of al-Anfal did not encompass them at that time. What barrier prevents that when the verse is absolute (مطلقة) and there is no restrictor (مقيد) to limit it?

It is strange that he admits their flight on those two days was a forbidden major sin, yet then says: "this does not necessitate that it is among those things whose perpetrator incurs wrath from Allah and whose abode is Hell—an evil destination; rather, it may be less than that," despite the fact that the destructive major sins (الكبائر الموبقة) are those acts of disobedience for which Allah has threatened the Fire.

Even stranger is his saying: "it is restricted by the verse of the license for weakness coming later in this Surah, and by the prohibition of throwing oneself into destruction in its generality!" For the verse of the license for weakness only indicates the permission to flee if the number of the enemy's advancing forces exceeds double.

And the verse prohibiting throwing oneself into destruction—if it were to indicate by its generality more than what the verse of the license for weakness indicates, the verse of al-Anfal would become redundant and remain without an instance of application (مصداق). Similarly, the interpretation of His saying: أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ "or withdrawing to a party" according to what the narration of Ibn 'Umar necessitates would lead to the nullification of the verse, as mentioned by the author of al-Manar. Thus, it is summarized that there is no escape from maintaining the verse upon its apparent absolute sense.

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): regarding the verse: إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ "unless it be to maneuver for battle," he said: "Moving away while intending to return to the attack against them." أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ "or withdrawing to a party," he said: "Meaning retreating to his companions without being defeated (هزيمة); whoever is defeated such that he passes the rank of his companions has indeed incurred wrath from Allah."

I say: The narration points to an important subtle point in the wording of the verse, which is that the prohibition in the verse is attached to "turning the backs" (تولي الأدبار), which is more general than "defeat" (الانهزام). So when the two cases are excepted—namely maneuvering for battle and withdrawing to a party, which are not cases of fleeing due to defeat—the cases of defeat remain under the prohibition. Thus, every defeat before the enemies of the religion, if they do not exceed double in number, is forbidden and prohibited.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahr Ashub from al-Tha'labi from Dahhak from 'Ikrimah from Ibn 'Abbas: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ "And you did not throw when you threw" (8:17), that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to 'Ali: "Hand me a handful of pebbles." He handed them to him, and he threw them into the faces of Quraysh; there remained no one but that his eyes were filled with pebbles.

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn 'Abbas. Al-'Ayyashi narrated in his Tafsir the hadith of the handing over from Muhammad ibn Kulayb al-Asadi from his father from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), and in another report from 'Ali (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir extracted from Muhammad ibn Qays and Muhammad ibn Ka'b (رضي الله عنهما) who both said: "When the people drew near to one another, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took a handful of dust and threw it into the faces of the people, saying: شاهت الوجوه — 'May the faces be deformed.' It entered the eyes of all of them, and the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) advanced, killing them. Their defeat was due to the throw of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). So Allah revealed: وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ to His saying سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ."

I say: The intent behind the "revelation of the verse" is its revelation after that, as it recounts the story, not its revelation at that exact moment; this is common in the occasions of revelation. Ibn Hisham mentioned in his Sirah: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) threw dust at them and then ordered his companions to charge, and thus was the defeat.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn Mandah, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il extracted from Ibn Shihab from 'Abdullah ibn Tha'labah ibn Su'ayr: that Abu Jahl said when the two groups met: "O Allah, [destroy] the one among us who has most severed the ties of kinship and brought us that which we do not recognize; so destroy him this morning." That was a request for a decision (استفتاحاً) from him; so there was revealed: إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ "If you seek a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you" (8:19).

In al-Majma': regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ "Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight..." (8:22). He said: al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: "They are the Banu 'Abd al-Dar; none of them had embraced Islam except Mus'ab ibn 'Umayr and an ally of theirs called Suwaybit."

In Jami' al-Jawami': al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: "They are the Banu 'Abd al-Dar; none of them embraced Islam except Mus'ab ibn 'Umayr and Suwayd ibn Harmalah. They used to say: 'We are deaf, dumb, and blind to what Muhammad has brought.' They were all killed at Uhud, and they were the standard-bearers."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur something to this effect through paths from Ibn 'Abbas and Qatadah. The narration is of the nature of application (الجري والانطباق), and the verse is general.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ. He said: "He said: Life (الحياة) is Paradise."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Rabi' al-Shami who said: I asked Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ. He said: "It was revealed concerning the guardianship (ولاية) of 'Ali (عليه‌السلام)."

I say: It was narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from Ibn Mardawayh from his men, raised to Imam Muhammad ibn 'Ali al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), and likewise from Abu al-Jarud from him (عليه‌السلام) as narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir. The narration is of the nature of application, as is the narration preceding it. We have already stated in the discussion on the verse that it is general.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from Abu al-Jarud from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ "and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart." He says: "Between a man and his disobedience, lest it lead him to the Fire; and He intervenes between the disbeliever and his obedience, lest he complete his faith through it. And know that deeds are judged by their endings."

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of transmission from 'Ali ibn al-Hakam from Hisham ibn Salim from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ. He said: "He intervenes between him and his knowing that falsehood is truth."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Ma'ani from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from Hisham ibn Salim from him (عليه‌السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Yunus ibn 'Ammar from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The heart never becomes certain that truth is falsehood, nor does it ever become certain that falsehood is truth."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn 'Abbas (رضي الله عنهما) who said: I asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about this verse: يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ. He said: "He intervenes between the believer and disbelief, and He intervenes between the disbeliever and guidance."

I say: This is close to the report mentioned previously from Abu al-Jarud from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) regarding the meaning of the verse.

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Hamzah al-Tayyar from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام): وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ. He said: "It is that he desires a thing with his hearing, his sight, his tongue, and his hand—yet he does not commit any of it even if he desires it—for he does not approach it except that his heart is disapproving, not accepting what he approaches; he knows that truth is not in it."

I say: Al-Barqi narrated it in al-Mahasin with his chain from Hamzah al-Tayyar from him (عليه‌السلام). Al-'Ayyashi narrated something close to it in his Tafsir from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام). The meaning of the narration goes back to the two narrations mentioned previously from Hisham ibn Salim and Yunus ibn 'Ammar from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Sayqal: Abu 'Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) was asked about وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً "And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively" (8:25). He said: "I was informed that they are the People of the Camel (أصحاب الجمل)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, he said: He said: "It was revealed concerning Talhah and al-Zubayr—when they fought the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) and wronged him."

In al-Majma', from al-Hakim with his chain from Qatadah from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib from Ibn 'Abbas who said: When this verse was revealed: وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Whoever wrongs 'Ali regarding this position of mine after my death—it is as if he has denied my prophethood and the prophethood of the prophets before me."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Nu'aym ibn Hammad in al-Fitan, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from al-Zubayr (رضي الله عنه) who said: "We recited it for a long time and did not think we were among its people—then suddenly we were the ones intended by it: وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً."

In it, Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from al-Suddi regarding the verse: "This was revealed concerning the people of Badr specifically—so it struck them on the Day of the Camel and they fought one another—and among those killed were Talhah and al-Zubayr, and they were from the people of Badr."

In it, Ahmad, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn 'Asakir extracted from Mutarrif who said: We said to al-Zubayr: "O Abu 'Abdullah, you neglected the Caliph until he was killed—then you came seeking his blood?" Al-Zubayr (رضي الله عنه) said: "We used to recite during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), Abu Bakr, 'Umar, and 'Uthman (رضي الله عنهم): وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً—and we did not think we were its people—until it fell upon us where it fell."

In it, 'Abd ibn Humayd and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Qatadah (رضي الله عنه) regarding the verse: "By Allah, the possessors of intellect among the companions of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) knew that there would be tribulations (فتن)."

In it, Abu al-Shaykh, Abu Nu'aym, and al-Daylami in Musnad al-Firdawsi extracted from Ibn 'Abbas (رضي الله عنهما) from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ أَنْتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تَخافُونَ أَنْ يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ. It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, and who are 'the people'?" He said: "The people of Persia."

I say: The narration does not harmonize with the context of the verse.

In it: regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ. Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Jabir ibn 'Abdullah (رضي الله عنه): that Abu Sufyan left Mecca—so Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "Indeed Abu Sufyan is in such-and-such a place—so go out against him and keep it secret." But a man from the hypocrites wrote to Abu Sufyan: "Indeed Muhammad intends [to attack] you, so take your precaution." So Allah revealed: لا تَخُونُوا اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ.

I say: The meaning of the narration is close in application to what we derived from the verse in the preceding commentary.

In it, Ibn Jarir extracted from al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah who said: "This verse was revealed concerning the killing of 'Uthman (رضي الله عنه)."

I say: The verse does not apply to it by its context at all.

In al-Majma', from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), al-Kalbi, and al-Zuhri: It was revealed concerning Abu Lubabah ibn 'Abd al-Mundhir al-Ansari. That was when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) besieged the Jews of Qurayzah for twenty-one nights. They asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for a peace treaty—upon what he had made peace with their brothers from the Banu al-Nadir—that they should travel to their brothers in Adhri'at and Arihat in the land of Syria. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) refused to grant them that—unless they submitted to the judgment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh. They said: "Send Abu Lubabah to us"—and he was a sincere advisor to them because his family, wealth, and children were with them. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent him. He came to them and they said: "What do you see, O Abu Lubabah? Should we submit to the judgment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh?" Abu Lubabah pointed with his hand to his throat: [meaning] that it is slaughter—so do not do it. Then Gabriel came to him and informed him of that.

Abu Lubabah said: "By Allah, my feet did not move from their place until I knew that I had betrayed Allah and His Messenger." So the verse was revealed concerning him. When it was revealed, he tied himself to one of the pillars of the mosque and said: "By Allah, I shall not taste food nor drink until I die or Allah turns to me in forgiveness." He remained for seven days without tasting food or drink until he fell unconscious. Then Allah turned to him in forgiveness. It was said to him: "O Abu Lubabah, you have been forgiven." He said: "No, by Allah, I shall not untie myself until the Messenger of Allah is the one who unties me." So he came to him and untied him with his hand.

Then Abu Lubabah said: "Indeed, part of the completion of my repentance is that I should leave the house of my people in which I committed the sin, and that I should divest myself of my wealth." The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "One-third is sufficient for you to give in charity."

I say: The story of Abu Lubabah and his repentance is authentic and capable of being applied to the content of the two verses, except that it occurred long after the story of Badr. The apparent sense of the two verses, when considered and compared to the verses preceding them, is that all are in a single context revealed shortly after the event of Badr. And Allah knows best.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِيُثْبِتُوكَ أَوْ يَقْتُلُوكَ أَوْ يُخْرِجُوكَ ۚ وَيَمْكُرُونَ وَيَمْكُرُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ (٣٠)
When the faithless plotted against you to take you captive, or to kill or expel you—they plotted and Allah devised, and Allah is the best of devisers. (30)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالُوا۟ قَدْ سَمِعْنَا لَوْ نَشَآءُ لَقُلْنَا مِثْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۙ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣١)
When Our signs are recited to them, they say, ‘We have heard already. If we want, we [too] can say like this. These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (31)
وَإِذْ قَالُوا۟ ٱللَّهُمَّ إِن كَانَ هَـٰذَا هُوَ ٱلْحَقَّ مِنْ عِندِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنَا حِجَارَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَوِ ٱئْتِنَا بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣٢)
And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be the truth from You, rain down upon us stones from the sky, or bring us a painful punishment.’ (32)
وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنتَ فِيهِمْ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (٣٣)
But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness. (33)
وَمَا لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٤)
What [excuse] have they that Allah should not punish them, when they bar [the faithful] from the Holy Mosque, and they are not its custodians? Its custodians are only the Godwary, but most of them do not know. (34)
وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكَآءً وَتَصْدِيَةً ۚ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٥)
Their prayer at the House is nothing but whistling and clapping. So taste the punishment because of what you used to defy. (35)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ لِيَصُدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَسَيُنفِقُونَهَا ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ (٣٦)
Indeed the faithless spend their wealth to bar from the way of Allah. Soon they will have spent it, then it will be a cause of regret to them, then they will be overcome, and the faithless will be gathered toward Hell, (36)
لِيَمِيزَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُۥ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُۥ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُۥ فِى جَهَنَّمَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٣٧)
so that Allah may separate the bad ones from the good, and place the bad on one another, and pile them up together, and cast them into hell. It is they who are the losers. (37)
قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَنتَهُوا۟ يُغْفَرْ لَهُم مَّا قَدْ سَلَفَ وَإِن يَعُودُوا۟ فَقَدْ مَضَتْ سُنَّتُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٨)
Say to the faithless, if they cease [their belligerence against the Muslims], what is already past shall be forgiven them. But if they resume [their hostilities], then the precedent of the predecessors has already come to pass. (38)
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ كُلُّهُۥ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٣٩)
Fight them until persecution is no more, and religion becomes exclusively for Allah. So if they desist, Allah indeed watches what they do. (39)
وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ (٤٠)
But if they turn away, then know that Allah is your master: an excellent master and an excellent helper! (40)

(Commentary)

The verses follow the context of the preceding verses and are connected to them, turning back toward the verses at the beginning of the Surah, except for His saying: وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَ (And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be indeed the truth...) and the verse following it. Indeed, the appearance of their connection is distinct from the rest of the verses, and the discussion regarding them will follow, if Allah (the Exalted) wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِيُثْبِتُوكَ أَوْ يَقْتُلُوكَ أَوْ يُخْرِجُوكَ (When the faithless plotted against you to captivate you, or to kill you, or to expel you...) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: Plotting (المكر) is to turn another away from what he intends through a ruse (حيلة). This is of two types: a praiseworthy type, which is to seek a beautiful act thereby, and regarding this He said: وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ (and Allah is the best of devisers); and a blameworthy type, which is to seek an ugly act thereby. He said: وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ (but the evil plot enmeshes only its authors), and وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (When the faithless plotted against you), and فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ (So observe how was the outcome of their plotting). He said regarding both matters: وَمَكَرُوا مَكْراً وَمَكَرْنا مَكْراً (They devised a plot, and We [too] devised a plot). Some have said: Among the plotting of Allah is granting the servant respite and enabling him to attain the vanities of the world. Therefore, the Commander of the Faithful (رضي الله عنه) said: "He for whom his worldly life is expanded and does not realize that he is being plotted against—he is deceived regarding his intellect." End quote.

In al-Majma': Captivating (الإثبات) means imprisonment (الحبس). It is said: "He shot him and athbatahu," meaning he confined him to his place. And "athbatahu the war," meaning it wounded him with a debilitating wound. End quote.

The requirement of the context of the verses is that His saying: وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (When the faithless plotted against you...) is conjoined to His previous saying: وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللهُ إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ أَنَّها لَكُمْ (When Allah promised you one of the two groups [attaining] which was to your advantage...). Thus, the verse is presented to explain the blessings Allah bestowed upon them and the favors with which He aided them, in which they had no hand.

The meaning of the verse is: And remember—or let them remember—when the disbelievers from Quraysh plotted against you to invalidate your call by inflicting upon you one of three matters: either that they imprison you, or that they kill you, or that they expel you. They plot, and Allah plots, and Allah is the best of plotters.

The hesitation in the verse between imprisonment, killing, and expulsion—as an explanation of the plot they were devising—indicates that there was a consultation (شورى) among them in which they consulted one another regarding the matter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and what concerned them and what they were preoccupied with regarding extinguishing the light of his call. By this, what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) is supported: that the verse refers to the story of Dar al-Nadwa, as will come in the following traditional discussion, if Allah (the Exalted) wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا قالُوا قَدْ سَمِعْنا لَوْ نَشاءُ لَقُلْنا مِثْلَ هذا (When Our signs are recited to them, they say, ‘We have heard [them] already. If we wanted, we could say the like of it...) to the end of the verse. Myths (الأساطير) are tales, the plural of usturah, and it is predominantly used for superstitious reports. His saying, narrating from them: قَدْ سَمِعْنا (We have heard), and His saying: لَوْ نَشاءُ لَقُلْنا (If we wanted, we could say), and His saying: مِثْلَ هذا (the like of this)—and He did not say "the like of these" or "their like"—all of this is to indicate their contempt for the signs of Allah and their disparagement of the station of the Message (الرسالة). Similar to it is their saying: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ (These are nothing but myths of the ancients).

The meaning is: When Our signs are recited to them—which leave no doubt in their indication that they are from Us, and which reveal what We desire from them of the true religion—they persist and transgress against them, belittling their matter and disparaging Our Message. They said: "We have heard and understood this which is recited to us; it has no reality except that it is from the myths of the ancients. If we wished, we could say the like of it, but we do not pay attention to it nor do we concern ourselves with such superstitious tales."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ (And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You...) to the end of the two verses. Raining down (الإمطار) is the sending down of something from above. It is predominantly used for drops of water from rain, or it is a metaphor of raining rain for other things like stones. In any case, their saying: "Rain down upon us stones from the sky," with the explicit mention of "the sky," is to indicate its being in the manner of a celestial sign and pure divine destruction.

Thus, the raining of stones from the sky upon them—according to what they requested—is one of the types of punishment (العذاب), and the rest remains under their saying: أَوِ ائْتِنا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ (or bring us a painful punishment). Therefore, "punishment" was made indefinite and its description was left vague to indicate the remaining types of punishment. The sum of the speech yields: "Rain down upon us stones from the sky or bring us another punishment besides it that is painful." Raining stones was singled out for mention from among the types of painful punishment because being crushed by stones combines the punishment of the body—due to the physical pain—and the punishment of the spirit—due to the humiliation and disgrace.

Furthermore, His saying: إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ (if this be indeed the truth from You) indicates by its wording that what they heard from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through the tongue of speech or state in his call was his saying: "This is the truth from Allah," and it contains something of the meaning of exclusivity (الحصر). This is different from what he used to say to them: "This is truth from Allah," for the second statement is addressed to one who does not recognize a celestial religion or a divine prophecy, as the polytheists (المشركون)—who were idolaters (الوثنية)—used to say: "Allah has not sent down anything to any human." As for the first statement, it is only addressed to one who sees that there is a true religion from Allah and a divine message that conveys the truth from Him, but then denies that what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought, or some of what he brought, is the truth from Allah (the Exalted). Thus, he is confronted with the fact that it is the truth from Allah and nothing else. Then, it is countered with the condition in a statement like: "O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky or bring us a painful punishment."

Therefore, it is more likely that this is not a narration from some of the polytheists attributed to all of them due to their agreement in opinion or the satisfaction of all of them with what this speaker said. Rather, it is as if it is a narration from some of the people of apostasy (أهل الردة) among those who submitted and then apostatized, or from some of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) who believe in a true celestial religion. So understand that.

This is supported by the verse following this one: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness). As for His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ (But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst), if what is intended by it is the negation of Allah's punishment of the disbelievers of Quraysh in Mecca before the Hijrah while the Prophet was among them, its meaning would be that the barrier to the descent of punishment then was the presence of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) among them. What is intended by punishment is other than the punishment that befell them at the hand of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of killing and captivity, as Allah called it "punishment" in the preceding verses and said in similar ones: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمُ اللهُ بِعَذابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينا (Say, ‘Do you await for us anything except one of the two best outcomes? But we await for you that Allah should visit you with a punishment from Himself or by our hands’) [9:52]. Rather, it refers to the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) by a celestial sign, as occurred with the nations of past prophets. However, Allah (the Glorified) threatened them with the punishment of extirpation in many verses, such as His saying: فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَقُلْ أَنْذَرْتُكُمْ صاعِقَةً مِثْلَ صاعِقَةِ عادٍ وَثَمُودَ (But if they turn away, say, ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud’) [41:13]. How do such threats harmonize with His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ (But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst) if the intended ones to be punished were the disbelievers of Quraysh and the polytheists of the Arabs as long as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in Mecca?

And if the intended ones to be punished were all the Arabs or the Ummah (الأمة), and the meaning of His saying: وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ (while you are in their midst) was the life of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—the meaning being: Allah will not punish this Ummah while you are among them alive, as might be supported by His saying after it: وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness)—that would be a negation of punishment for the entire Ummah and would not contradict its descent upon some of them, just as the occurrence of killing among them was called "punishment" in the preceding verses, and as it has been narrated that Allah (the Exalted) punished a group of them like Abu Lahab and those who mocked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Based on this, the verse would not include those who said: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ (O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You...) to the end of the verse, especially considering what is narrated that the one who said it was Abu Jahl—as in Sahih al-Bukhari—or al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Kalada—as in some other narrations—and the word of punishment was fulfilled against both of them and they were killed on the day of Badr. Thus, the verse وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ (But Allah will not punish them...) would not be connected to these speakers of اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ (O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You...) even though it is presented in the manner of a response to their statement.

The problem intensifies based on what occurs in some occasions of revelation: that they said, "O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky or bring us a painful punishment," so His saying (the Exalted) was revealed: سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِعٍ لِلْكافِرينَ لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ (A questioner asked for a punishment bound to happen, for the faithless, which none can avert). The discussion regarding this and other narrated occasions of revelation will follow in the next traditional discussion, if Allah wills.

As for what some exegetes have labored with in justifying the content of the verse—based on interpreting it according to the aforementioned meaning—that Allah (the Glorified) sent Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as a mercy to all the worlds (رحمة للعالمين) and a blessing for this Ummah, not as a vengeance and punishment: in it is that the requirement of being a mercy to all the worlds is not to neglect the interest of the religion, nor to remain silent regarding the injustices of the wrongdoers even if they reach what they reach and lead to the misery of the righteous and the disorder of the system of the world and religion. Allah (the Glorified) has narrated about Himself by His saying: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ (and My mercy embraces all things), yet that did not prevent His wrath from descending upon those past nations and bygone generations upon whom it descended, as He mentioned in His speech.

Furthermore, He (the Exalted) called what befell the disbelievers of Quraysh of killing and destruction at Badr and elsewhere "punishment," and that did not contradict His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ (We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds) [21:107]. He threatened this Ummah with a certain, occurring punishment in the Surahs of Yunus, al-Isra, al-Anbiya, al-Qasas, al-Rum, al-Ma'arij, and others, and that did not contradict his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being a mercy to all the worlds. So why would the descent of punishment upon a small group that uttered this word: اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَ (O Allah, if this be indeed the truth...) contradict the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being the Prophet of Mercy, while it is from the requirements of mercy that every possessor of a right be given his right, that the wronged be retaliated for against the wrongdoer, and that every tyrant be taken for his tyranny?

As for His saying (the Exalted): وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness), its apparent meaning is the future negation (النفي الاستقبالي) according to what is apparent from the attribute (الصفة): مُعَذِّبَهُمْ (punishing them), and His saying: يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (they plead for forgiveness) being presented to convey continuity (الاستمرار) and the sentence being circumstantial (حالية). The meaning is: Allah will not face them with punishment as long as they seek His forgiveness.

The verse, however it is taken, does not apply to the state of the polytheists of Mecca while they were polytheists and obstinate, not submitting to truth nor seeking forgiveness for an injustice or a crime. The matter cannot be rectified by what has been narrated in some reports that they said what they said and then regretted what they said and sought Allah's forgiveness by their saying: "Your forgiveness, O Allah."

That is—in addition to its lack of proof—because He (the Exalted) does not pay heed in His speech to the seeking of forgiveness by the polytheists, especially the leaders of disbelief (أئمة الكفر) among them. Futile seeking of forgiveness has no effect. If their seeking of forgiveness were not futile and what they committed by their saying: "O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky..." were removed by it, there would be no reason for their censure and reprimand by His saying (the Exalted): وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَ (And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be indeed the truth...) in the context of these verses presented to censure them, blame them, and count their crimes and injustices against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers.

Moreover, His saying (the Exalted) after the two verses: وَما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ (What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them, when they bar [the faithful] from the Sacred Mosque...) does not harmonize with the negation of punishment in these two verses. For the apparent meaning of the verse is that the punishment threatened is the punishment of killing at the hands of the believers, as indicated by His saying after it: فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve). At that point, if the speakers of اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَ (O Allah, if this be indeed the truth...) were the polytheists of Quraysh or some of them, and the intended punishment negated was the celestial punishment, it would not be sound to deny the occurrence of punishment upon them by killing and the like. For the speech then would lead to the meaning of intensification (التشديد):

Its summary being: they were more deserving of punishment and they have another crime beyond what they committed, which is barring from the Sacred Mosque. This type of progression (الترقي) is more suitable for affirming punishment for them, not for negating it from them.

And if the intended punishment negated was killing and the like, the lack of harmony between His saying: وَما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللهُ (What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them) and His saying: فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve), and between His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ (But Allah will not punish them...) etc., would be clearer and more apparent.

Some have perhaps justified the verse with this meaning by saying that what is intended by His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ (But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst) is the punishment of the people of Mecca before the Hijrah, and by His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness) the punishment of the people entirely after his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migration to Medina and the faith of a group and their seeking of forgiveness. Therefore, it was said: the beginning of the verse was revealed before the Hijrah, and its end after the Hijrah!

This is clearly invalid. For when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them in Mecca before the Hijrah, there was with him a group of those who believed in Allah and sought His forgiveness. And he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after the Hijrah was among the people. So what is the meaning of specifying the beginning of the verse with His saying: وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ (while you are in their midst) and its end with His saying: وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (while they plead for forgiveness)?

And if it were assumed that the meaning of the verse is that Allah does not punish this Ummah as long as you are among them by the blessing of your presence, and He does not punish them after you by the blessing of their seeking forgiveness from Allah—and the intended punishment is the punishment of extirpation—it would not harmonize with the two following verses: وَما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللهُ (What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them...) etc., along with what has preceded of the problem regarding it.

Thus, it has become clear from all that has preceded—despite its length—that the two verses, namely His saying: وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنا حِجارَةً (And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones...) to the end of the two verses, do not share with the preceding and following verses—which are narrated in the discourse regarding the disbelievers of Quraysh—their single context. Thus, they were not revealed with them.

The most likely view is that what is narrated in them of their saying and the response to it by His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ (But Allah will not punish them...) is not connected to them. Rather, this statement was issued by some of the People of the Book or some of those who believed and then apostatized from among the people.

This is supported by some of what has been narrated that the speaker of this statement was al-Harith ibn al-Nu'man al-Fihri. The narration has already preceded, quoted from the two commentaries of al-Tha'labi and al-Majma', under His saying (the Exalted): يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord...) [5:67] in the sixth volume of the book.

According to this estimation, what is intended by the negated punishment is the celestial punishment followed by the comprehensive extirpation of the Ummah, in the manner of the punishment of other nations. Allah (the Glorified) negates therein the punishment from the Ummah as long as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is among them alive, and after him as long as they seek Allah's (the Exalted) forgiveness.

It appears from His saying (the Exalted): وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness)—by joining it to the verses that threaten this Ummah with the punishment that judges between the Messenger and them, like the verses of Surah Yunus: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ (There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged) [10:47] to the end of the verses—that in the future of this Ummah's affair, there is a day when seeking forgiveness will be cut off from them and the divine believer will be removed from among them, and they will be punished at that time.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ (What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them, when they bar [the faithful] from the Sacred Mosque, though they are not its custodians?...) to the end of the verse. It is an interrogative in the sense of denial (الإنكار) or wonder (التعجب). His saying: وَما لَهُمْ (What [plea] have they) assumes a verb to which the prepositional phrase relates, and His saying: أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ (that He should not punish them) is its object, or it is from the implication (التضمين) similar to what was said regarding His saying: هَلْ لَكَ إِلى أَنْ تَزَكَّى (Would you like to purify yourself?) [79:18].

The estimation in any case is something like our saying: "And what is it that establishes and justifies for them Allah's not punishing them, while the state is that they bar from the Sacred Mosque and prevent the believers from entering it (وَما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ) (though they are not its custodians)?" So His saying: وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ (when they bar...) etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the pronoun in يُعَذِّبَهُمُ (punish them), and His saying: وَما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ (though they are not its custodians) is a circumstantial clause for the pronoun in يَصُدُّونَ (they bar).

His saying: إِنْ أَوْلِياؤُهُ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقُونَ (Its custodians are only the God-conscious) is a justification (تعليل) for His saying: وَما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ (though they are not its custodians). That is, they have no right to oversee the affair of the House, permitting and preventing whom they wish, because this Mosque is built upon the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah, so none oversees its affair except the God-conscious (المتقون), and they are not among them.

Thus, His saying: إِنْ أَوْلِياؤُهُ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقُونَ (Its custodians are only the God-conscious) is an informative sentence (جملة خبرية) that justifies the statement with an evident matter perceived by every possessor of intellect. The sentence is not performative (إنشائية) comprising the appointment of custodianship for the God-conscious. What we have mentioned is evidenced by His saying after: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (but most of them do not know), as is not hidden.

What is intended by punishment is the punishment by killing or what is more general than it, according to what the context provides by the connection of the verse to the following verse. It has already preceded that the verse is apparently not connected to what preceded it—meaning that the two verses: وَإِذْ قالُوا اللهُمَ (And when they said, ‘O Allah...) etc., and وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ (But Allah will not punish them...) etc., are outside the context of the verses—and the necessity of that is what we have mentioned.

He said in al-Majma': It is asked: How are the two verses reconciled when in the first there is a negation of their punishment, and in the second an affirmation of it? The answer to it is from three perspectives:

One of them: That what is intended by the first is the punishment of eradication (الاصطلام) and extirpation (الاستئصال) as was done with past nations, and by the second the punishment of killing by the sword, captivity, and other than that after the departure of the believers from among them.

The other: That He intended: "What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them in the Hereafter," and He intends by the first the punishment of the world. From al-Jubba'i.

The third: That the first is an invitation to seek forgiveness. He intends that He will not punish them with the punishment of the world nor the Hereafter if they seek forgiveness and repent; so if they do not do so, they are punished. Then He explained that their deserving of punishment is due to their barring from the Sacred Mosque. End quote.

In it is: that the basis of the problem is the connection of the verse to what is before it, and it has already preceded that it is not connected. This is in general.

As for in detail, it is countered regarding the first perspective: that the context of the verse—which is, as preceded, a context of intensification and progression—does not harmonize with the negation of punishment in the first with its affirmation in the second, even if the punishment is other than the punishment.

Regarding the second: that the context of the verse contradicts the intended punishment therein being the punishment of the Hereafter, especially in view of His saying in the third verse—which is in the context of the first verse—فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve).

Regarding the third: that this is undoubtedly contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, since its apparent meaning is the affirmation of seeking forgiveness for them as a continuous state due to its invitation, and that is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ صَلاتُهُمْ عِنْدَ الْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكاءً وَتَصْدِيَةً فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (Their prayer at the House is nothing but whistling and clapping. So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve). Whistling (المكاء) with a damma on the mim is whistling. Al-muka' in the intensive form is a bird in the Hijaz with a loud whistle. From it is the common proverb: "Your sons are humari and your muka'ikini." And clapping (التصدية) is clapping by striking hand upon hand.

His saying: وَما كانَ صَلاتُهُمْ (Their prayer was nothing but...) the pronoun refers to these barring ones mentioned in the previous verse, and they are the polytheists of Quraysh. His saying: فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (So taste the punishment because of what you used to disbelieve) is an explanation of the fulfillment of the punishment promised to them, by the evidence of the derivation with the fa'.

From here, it is supported that the two verses are connected as a single speech. His saying: وَما كانَ (And was not...) etc., is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية). The meaning is: What [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them while the state is that they bar the servants among the believers from the Sacred Mosque, and their prayer at the House was nothing but a play of whistling and clapping? So if it is so, then let them taste the punishment because of what they used to disbelieve. The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ (So taste the punishment) from the third person to the second person is for the sake of reaching intensification.

It is benefited from the two verses that if the Noble Kaaba were left to be barred, that would be followed by divine reckoning with punishment. Ali (peace be upon him) said in some of his testaments: "Allah, Allah, regarding the House of your Lord, for indeed if it is left, you will not be looked upon (١)."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ لِيَصُدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ (Indeed the faithless spend their wealth to bar [people] from the way of Allah...) to the end of the verse. It explains the state of the disbelievers in the error of their effort which they exert to invalidate the call of Allah and to prevent the wayfarers from treading the path of Allah. This is explained by His saying: فَسَيُنْفِقُونَها ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ (They will surely spend it, then it will become a regret for them, then they will be overcome...) etc.

By this context, it appears that His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ (and the faithless shall be mustered toward hell) is in the position of justification. The summary of the meaning is that disbelief will incite them—according to the sunnah (سنة) of Allah in causes—to strive in invalidating the call and barring from the path of truth. However, injustice, transgression (الفسق), and all corruption do not lead to success and prosperity. So they will spend their wealth in the way of these corrupt goals, and the wealth will be wasted in this path, so its waste will be a cause for their regret. Then they will be overcome, so they will not benefit from it. That is because the disbelievers are mustered toward Hell, and what they do in the world of gathering for evil and going out to fight Allah and His Messenger is in correspondence to their going out mustered toward Hell on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying: فَسَيُنْفِقُونَها ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ (They will surely spend it, then it will become a regret for them, then they will be overcome...) to the end of the verse is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran. The verse is from Surah al-Anfal, revealed after the Battle of Badr, so it is as if it refers to what will occur in the Battle of Uhud, or it and others. Based on this, His saying: فَسَيُنْفِقُونَها ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً (They will surely spend it, then it will become a regret for them) is an indication of the Battle of Uhud, or it and others. His saying: ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ (then they will be overcome) refers to the Conquest of Mecca. His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ (and the faithless shall be mustered toward hell) refers to the state of those among them who are not granted the success of Islam.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ (so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell. It is they who are the losers). Badness (الخباثة) and goodness (الطيبة) are two opposite meanings, and their explanation has already passed. Separation (التمييز) is the removal of a thing from what opposes it and its joining with what accords with it, such that it is separated from what opposes it. Heaping (الركم) is the gathering of a thing upon a thing. From it is "heaped cloud (سحاب مركوم)," meaning its parts are gathered one to another and their sum. The accumulation (تراكم) of things is the piling of some upon others.

The verse is in the position of justification for what He informed of in the previous verse regarding the state of the disbelievers according to the cosmic sunnah (السنة الكونية). It is that they strive with all their diligence and capability to extinguish the light of Allah and bar from the path of Allah, so they spend wealth in that and exert efforts in its path. However, they are not guided to their objectives nor do they reach their hopes; rather, their wealth is wasted, their deeds are nullified, and their efforts go astray, and they inherit thereby regret and defeat.

That is because these deeds and fluctuations proceed according to a divine sunnah and are directed toward a cosmic, lordly goal. It is that Allah (the Glorified) separates in this ongoing system the evil from the good and the bad from the good, and He heaps the bad by placing some of it upon others, and He places what has gathered of it and accumulated in Hell. It is the goal toward which the caravan of evil and the bad proceeds, and all of them settle in it, and it is the abode of ruin (دار البوار), just as the good and the pure are toward Paradise. The former are the losers, just as the latter are the successful winners.

From here, it appears that His saying: لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ (so that Allah may separate the bad from the good...) etc., is close in content to His saying (the Exalted) in a parable He struck for truth and falsehood: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَأَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ (He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to their capacity, and the flood carries off a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire to make ornaments or wares, there is a scum like it. Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth) [13:17]. The verse refers to a universal divine law, which is the joining of the branch of every thing to its root.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَنْتَهُوا يُغْفَرْ لَهُمْ ما قَدْ سَلَفَ (Say to the faithless that if they desist, their past shall be forgiven them...) to the end of the verse. Desisting (الانتهاء) is the ceasing from a thing because of a prohibition. Passing (السلوف) is precedence. Sunnah (السنة) is the way and the conduct.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to convey that to them, and in its meaning is enticement and intimidation. Its reality is a call to abandon fighting and sedition (الفتنة) so that Allah may forgive them thereby what has preceded of their killing and harming of the believers. If they do not desist from what they were prohibited from, then the sunnah of Allah has already passed regarding the ancients among them of destruction, annihilation, and the loss of effort.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ لِلَّهِ فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا فَإِنَّ اللهَ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (Fight them until faithlessness is no more, and religion becomes exclusively for Allah. So if they desist, Allah indeed watches what they do). The verse and what follows it comprise the duty of the believers in correspondence to what the disbelievers were charged with in the previous verse. The meaning is: Say to them that if they desist from opposition to Allah and His Messenger, what has passed will be forgiven them; and if they return to the like of what they did, they have already known what befell their predecessors. Say to them such, but as for you and the believers, do not weaken in what concerns you of establishing the religion and purifying a righteous atmosphere for the believers. Fight them until these seditions (الفتن) that surprise you every day end, and there be no sedition thereafter. If they desist, then Allah will recompense them for what He sees of their deeds. And if they turn away from desisting, then continue the fighting, and Allah is your Master (مولاكم), so know that and do not weaken nor fear.

Sedition (الفتنة) is that by which souls are tested, and it is inevitably among what is difficult for them. Its usage became predominant for battles, the loss of security, and the breaking of peace. The disbelievers of Quraysh used to seize those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before the Hijrah and for a period after it in Mecca, punishing them and forcing them to abandon Islam and return to disbelief, and that was called fitna.

It has become clear by what the context provides of the previous meaning that His saying: وَقاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ (Fight them until faithlessness is no more) is a metaphor for weakening them through fighting until they are not deceived by their disbelief nor do they cast sedition by which the believers are tempted, and the religion becomes entirely for Allah, with no one calling to its contrary. And that His saying: فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا فَإِنَّ اللهَ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (So if they desist, Allah indeed watches what they do), what is intended by it is desisting from fighting. Therefore, He followed it with the like of His saying: فَإِنَّ اللهَ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (Allah indeed watches what they do), meaning at that point Allah will judge regarding them what suits their deeds, and He is watchful of them. And that His saying: وَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا (And if they turn away...) etc., meaning if they turn away from desisting, and do not cease from fighting nor abandon sedition, then know that Allah is your Master and your Helper, and fight them confident in the help of Allah—an excellent Master and an excellent Helper.

It has become clear that His saying: وَيَكُونَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ لِلَّهِ (and religion becomes exclusively for Allah) does not contradict the confirmation of the People of the Book upon their religion if they enter into the protection (الذمة) and give the jizya. Thus, there is no relationship for the verse with His saying (the Exalted): حَتَّى يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَنْ يَدٍ وَهُمْ صاغِرُونَ (until they pay the tribute out of hand, being humbled) [9:29] in terms of abrogation (الناسخية والمنسوخية).

Some exegetes have perspectives on the meaning of desisting, forgiveness, and others among the individual terms of the three verses, in which there is not much benefit in addressing, so we have left them.

It has been narrated in some reports that "the Excellent Master and the Excellent Helper" are among the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) of Allah. What is intended by the name then is inevitably other than the name in its technical sense, but rather everything that specifies by its wording some of the instances, as has been narrated similarly regarding His saying (the Exalted): لا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَلا نَوْمٌ (Neither drowsiness befalls Him nor sleep). The full discussion on the Beautiful Names has already passed under His saying (the Exalted): وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى (To Allah belong the Best Names) [7:180] in the eighth volume of the book.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying (the Exalted): وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (When the faithless plotted against you...) the verse—that it was revealed in Mecca before the Hijrah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ibn Jurayj (رض): وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (When the faithless plotted against you), he said: It is Meccan.

I say: This is the apparent meaning of what he also narrated from 'Abd ibn Humayd from Mu'awiya ibn Qurra, but you have known that the context of the verses does not support it.

In it, 'Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il, and al-Khatib narrated from Ibn 'Abbas (رضي الله عنهما): Regarding His saying: وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِيُثْبِتُوكَ (When the faithless plotted against you to captivate you), he said: Quraysh consulted one night in Mecca—so some of them said: "When it is morning, then captivate him with bonds"—intending the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and some of them said: "Rather, kill him," and some of them said: "Rather, expel him." So Allah informed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of that—so Ali (رضي الله عنه) spent the night on the bed of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out until he reached the cave. The polytheists spent the night guarding Ali (رضي الله عنه)—thinking him to be the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When it was morning, they rushed upon him—so when they saw him to be Ali (رضي الله عنه), Allah returned their plot—so they said: "Where is this companion of yours?" He said: "I do not know." So they tracked his footprints. When they reached the mountain—it became confused for them, so they climbed the mountain—then they saw on its door the web of a spider—so they said: "If he had entered here, there would not be the web of a spider on its door"—so he remained for three nights.
