Furthermore, a society, even if its members are few, is not free from differences in faith and beautiful spiritual traits, between the strong and the weak. Whenever the individuals increase, the weak in faith, those in whose hearts is a disease, and the hypocrites increase. Thus, the spiritual powers in the average individual decline, and the scale of the balance rises from the weight it once had.

Religious groups and worldly parties are equal in this regard. The natural way (السنة الطبيعية) that proceeds in the human system applies to all in the same manner. Definitive experience has proven that united societies for an important goal, whenever their members are few and their rivals and competitors are strong, and trials and tribulations surround them, they are more active in work and sharper in effect. And whenever their members increase and their competitors and the obstacles between them and their goals and objectives decrease, they are more subsided, less vigilant, and more foolish in judgment.

Sufficient reflection on the military expeditions (مغازي) of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) illuminates this. This is the Battle of Badr, in which the Muslims—who were three hundred and some odd men, despite their poor condition, lack of equipment, and absence of weapons and power—overcame the disbelievers of the Quraysh, who were three times the number of the Muslims or more, despite their pride, might, and power. Then there is what happened to the Muslims in the Battle of Uhud, then in the Battle of the Trench (الخندق), then in the Battle of Khaybar, then in the Battle of Hunayn—which is the most amazing of them. Allah, the Glorified, mentioned it in a way that leaves no doubt for a researcher, as He said: «وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ» "and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number tickled your fancy, but did not avail you anything, and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse; then you turned your backs [to flee]." (9:25) to the end of the verses.

The verse indicates, firstly, that as Islam increased in outward might and power during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it increased in inward deficiency and subsidence in the general spiritual powers of the Muslims and the degree of their faith and beautiful, spiritual, internal traits. This continued until it settled after the Battle of Badr—by a little or a lot—at one-fifth of what it was before it, as indicated by some allusion in the saying of the Exalted in the following verses: «ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ الدُّنْيا وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ لَوْ لا كِتابٌ مِنَ اللهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَخَذْتُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ» "A prophet may not take captives until he has fully established his hold on the land. You desire the transitory goods of this world, while Allah desires the Hereafter [for you], and Allah is All-mighty, All-wise. Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah, there would have surely befallen you a great punishment for what you took." (8:67-68).

Secondly: It appears that the two verses were revealed at once. For although they inform about the condition of the believers in two different times—as indicated by His saying in the second verse: "Now Allah has lightened it for you"—the two verses compare, as mentioned, the nature of the believers' spiritual powers in two different times. The context of the second, in view of this comparison, is such that it is not independent of the first. The existence of two different rulings in two times does not necessitate that the verse containing one of them was revealed at a time other than the time of the revelation of the other containing the second.

Yes, if the two verses were restricted to clarifying the legislative ruling (الحكم التكليفي) only, it would appear that the second was revealed after a time in which the first was revealed.

Thirdly: The appearance of His saying, the Exalted: "Now Allah has lightened it for you"—as has been said—is that the two verses are aimed at clarifying the legislative ruling, because lightening (التخفيف) only occurs after an obligation (التكليف). Thus, the wording is that of a report (خبر), but the intended meaning is a command (أمر). The gist of the meaning in the first verse is: let one of you stand firm against ten of the disbelievers; and in the second verse: now Allah has lightened His command, so let one of you stand firm against two of the disbelievers.

The restriction of "lightening" to the chapter of obligations—as has been said—although it can be debated, the appearance of the two verses in the existence of two different rulings, sequential in time, one of which is lighter than the other, should not be doubted.

Fourthly: The appearance of the justification in the first verse by "understanding" (الفقه), and in the second verse by "patience" (الصبر), while qualifying the fighter from among the believers in both verses with patience, indicates that patience outweighs the one in spiritual power over two like him, and understanding outweighs him in it over five like him. So when they meet in one person, he outweighs ten times himself. Patience does not separate from understanding, although the reverse is possible.

Fifthly: Patience is obligatory in fighting in any case.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In the Tafsir of al-Baydawi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتَ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِي كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ» "Those with whom you made a treaty, and who then break their treaty every time": They are the Jews of Banu Qurayzah. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) made a treaty with them that they would not aid anyone against him, but they aided the polytheists with weapons—and said: "We forgot." Then he made a treaty with them, and they broke it—and aided them against him on the Day of the Trench (الخندق). Ka‘b ibn al-Ashraf rode to Mecca and allied with them.

I say: This has been narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas and Mujahid. It is narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr that the verse was revealed concerning six individuals from the Jews, among them Ibn Tabut. The clarification of what the verse alludes to regarding the Jews' breaking of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) covenant time after time, and the hardships he endured from their side, requires a general survey of what occurred between him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and them from the time of his migration (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Medina until seven years after the Hijrah.

Groups of Jews had migrated from their lands to the Hijaz and settled there, building forts and castles. Their numbers increased, their wealth grew, and their affair became significant. Narrations regarding the beginning of their migration to the Hijaz, how they settled around Medina, and their giving glad tidings to the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) have already been mentioned under His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ كِتابٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ وَكانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ فَلَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ» "And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming what was with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the faithless—so when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may Allah’s curse be on the faithless!" (2:89) in the first volume of the book.

When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to Medina and called them to Islam, they disdained to believe in him. So he made peace with the Jews of Medina and entered into a treaty with them through a document written between him and them. They were three groups around Medina: Banu Qaynuqa‘, Banu al-Nadir, and Banu Qurayzah. As for Banu Qaynuqa‘, they broke the covenant during the Battle of Badr. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) marched to them in the middle of Shawwal in the second year of the Hijrah, twenty-some days after the event of Badr. They took refuge in their forts, so he besieged them with a severe siege, and they remained so for fifteen days.

Then they surrendered to the judgment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding their lives, wealth, women, and progeny. He ordered them to be bound. ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul spoke to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding them and persisted, as they were his allies. So he granted them to him and ordered them to leave Medina and not to be his neighbors there. They left for Adhri‘at in Syria, with their women and progeny, and he seized their wealth as spoils of war (غنيمة الحرب). They were six hundred fighters from the bravest of the Jews.

As for Banu al-Nadir, they plotted against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he went out to them with a group of his companions months after the Battle of Badr. He spoke to them to assist him in the blood money (دية) of a group or two men from the Kilabites whom ‘Amr ibn Umayyah al-Damri had killed. They said: "We will do so, O Abu al-Qasim. Sit here until we fulfill your need." They went into private council and conspired to kill him. They chose from among them ‘Amr ibn Juhash to take a millstone, climb up, and throw it on his head to crush it. Sallam ibn Mishkam warned them, saying: "Do not do that! By Allah, he will surely be informed of what you have intended, and it is a violation of the covenant between us and him."

Revelation came to him, and his Lord informed him of what they had intended. So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) rose from his seat quickly and headed toward Medina. His companions followed him and inquired about his rising and departure. He informed them of what Banu al-Nadir had intended. He sent word to them from Medina: "Leave Medina and do not live with me in it. I have given you a grace period; whoever I find after that among you, I will strike his neck." They stayed for days preparing to leave.

The hypocrite ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy sent word to them: "Do not leave your homes, for I have two thousand who will enter your fort with you and die for you. Banu Qurayzah and your allies from Ghatafan will help you." He satisfied them with that.

So their leader, Huyayy ibn Akhtab, sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "We will not leave our homes, so do what you see fit." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said "Allahu Akbar" and his companions said "Allahu Akbar." He ordered ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) to carry the banner and march to them. They marched and surrounded their homes. ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy betrayed them, and neither Banu Qurayzah nor their allies from Ghatafan helped them.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had ordered the cutting and burning of their palm trees, which caused them great distress. They said: "O Muhammad, do not cut them; if they are yours, then take them, and if they are ours, then leave them for us." After a few days, they said to him: "O Muhammad, we will leave your land, so give us our wealth." He replied: "No, but you shall leave and take only what your camels can carry." They refused this and remained as they were for some days. Later, they relented and asked him for that same deal, but he said: "No, but you shall leave and none of you shall carry anything; whomever we find with anything of it, we shall kill him." So they departed; some went to Fadak and Wadi al-Qura, while others went to the land of ash-Sham. Their wealth became spoils (فيئاً) for Allah and His Messenger without the Muslim army having to strike a blow for it. Their story is mentioned in Surah al-Hashr. Among the plots of Banu al-Nadir against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the mobilization of the Confederates (الأحزاب) from Quraysh, Ghatafan, and others against him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

As for Banu Qurayzah, they remained in a state of truce and peace until the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الخندق) occurred. Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the chief of Banu al-Nadir, had traveled to Makkah and incited Quraysh against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and organized the Confederates. During that time, he rode to Banu Qurayzah and came to them in their homes. He continued whispering to them, encouraging them, and persisting until he spoke to their chief, Ka'b ibn Asad, regarding the matter and the breaking of the covenant and engaging the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in battle, until he satisfied them with that. They stipulated that he must enter their fortress so that whatever befell them would befall him; he accepted and entered.

Thus, they broke the covenant and leaned toward the Confederates who were besieging Madinah. They openly insulted the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and created another breach.

When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) finished with the matter of the Confederates, Gabriel came to him with a revelation from Allah commanding him to march against them. He set out toward them, with Ali (عليه‌السلام) carrying his banner, and besieged the fortresses of Banu Qurayzah for twenty-five days.

When the siege became severe for them, their chief, Ka'b ibn Asad, proposed that they choose one of three options: either they embrace Islam and enter the religion of Muhammad; or they kill their children and go out to him with their swords drawn to fight him until they either triumph or are killed to the last man; or they launch a surprise attack on him on the Sabbath, because they—meaning the Muslims—would feel secure that they would not be fought on that day.

They refused to agree to any of these options. They sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asking him to send Abu Lubabah ibn Abd al-Mundhir so they could consult him on the matter; Abu Lubabah was a sincere advisor to them because his family, children, and wealth were with them.

He sent him to them. When they saw him, they stood up to him weeping and said: "How do you see our surrendering to the judgment of Muhammad?" He said: "Yes," and pointed with his hand to his throat, indicating slaughter. Abu Lubabah said: "By Allah, my feet had not moved before I realized that I had betrayed Allah and His Messenger." Allah then sent revelation to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the matter of Abu Lubabah.

Abu Lubabah felt deep remorse and went straight to the Mosque, where he tied himself to one of its pillars, repenting to Allah. He swore that no one should untie him except the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or he would die. When this reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "Leave him until Allah accepts his repentance." Subsequently, Allah accepted his repentance and revealed his forgiveness, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) untied him.

Thereafter, Banu Qurayzah surrendered to the judgment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They were allies of the Aws tribe, so the Aws spoke to him regarding them, seeking intercession (مستشفعين). The matter concluded with the appointment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh al-Awsi to judge their case. They agreed to this, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) also agreed. Sa'd was brought forth while he was wounded.

When Sa'd (رحمه الله) was spoken to regarding their matter, he said: "The time has come for Sa'd that he should not be affected, for the sake of Allah, by the blame of any blamer." He then judged that the men be killed, the women and children be taken captive, and the wealth be seized. What Sa'd judged was carried out upon them; their necks were struck to the last man. They were six hundred or seven hundred fighters, and it is said more. None of them survived except a few who believed before their execution. Amr ibn Su'da escaped from them, as he had not joined them in breaking the covenant. The women were taken captive except for one woman who was executed; she was the one who had thrown a millstone onto the head of Khallad ibn al-Suwayd ibn al-Samit (رحمه الله), killing him.

Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) expelled those Jews who remained in Madinah. Afterward, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) marched toward the Jews of Khaybar due to their plotting and their efforts in inciting the Confederates against him and their assembling of all the Arab tribes to fight him. He besieged their fortresses for several days. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent Abu Bakr with a group one day to fight them, but he was defeated; then he sent Umar ibn al-Khattab with a group another day, and he was defeated.

At that point, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Tomorrow I shall certainly give the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger—and whom Allah and His Messenger love—a relentless attacker who never flees; he shall not return until Allah grants victory at his hands." On the following day, he gave the banner to Ali (عليه‌السلام) and sent him to fight the people. He advanced toward them and killed Marhab, their famous knight, and defeated them. He wrenched off the gate of their fortress with his hand, and Allah granted victory at his hands. This occurred after the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah in Muharram of the seventh year of the Hijrah.

Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) expelled the remaining Jews, having advised them before that to sell their properties and take their prices. This concludes what we intended to summarize regarding the story of the Jews with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Jabir: regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ "Indeed the worst of beasts in the sight of Allah..." [Qarai 8:55]—the verse was revealed concerning the Umayyads; they are the worst of Allah's creation. They are الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "those who disbelieve" in the inner meaning (باطن) of the Quran, and they are الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "those who do not believe."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated something similar from Abu Hamzah from him (عليه‌السلام), and it is from the inner meaning (باطن) of the Quran as explicitly stated in the narration, not the apparent meaning (الظاهر).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Sahl ibn Ziyad from some of his companions from Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Three traits, if found in a person, make him a hypocrite—even if he fasts, prays, and claims to be a Muslim: he who betrays when entrusted, lies when he speaks, and breaks his word when he promises.' Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said in His Book: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ 'Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous' [Qarai 8:58], and He said: أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ '...that the curse of Allah be upon him if he is one of the liars' [Qarai 24:7]. And regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ ـ إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا 'And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise, and he was an apostle and a prophet' [Qarai 19:54]."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ "Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power..." [Qarai 8:60]—he said: "He (عليه‌السلام) said: 'Weapons (السلاح).'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Isa from someone who mentioned it from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the verse, he said: "A sword and a shield."

In al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) in a detached (مرسلاً) narration: regarding the verse, he said: "Included in it is dyeing [the hair] with black."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): A group of people entered upon al-Husayn ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام) and saw him dyed with black. They asked him about that, so he extended his hand to his beard and said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded in a battle he fought that they should dye [their hair] with black, so they might appear stronger against the polytheists."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Jabir al-Ansari, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding: وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ 'Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power'—'It is archery (الرمي).'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi with its chain from Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah in a raised (رفعه) report from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); by al-Zamakhshari in Rabi' al-Abrar from Uqbah ibn Amir from him; and by al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ahmad, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Abu Ya'qub Ishaq ibn Ibrahim, and al-Bayhaqi from Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi extracted from Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani (رضي الله عنه), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, Allah admits three people into Paradise because of a single arrow: its maker who seeks reward in his craftsmanship, the one who prepares it in the way of Allah, and the one who shoots it in the way of Allah.'"

And he said: "Shoot and ride, and that you shoot is better than that you ride." And he said: "Everything with which a son of Adam amuses himself is vain except three: his shooting with his bow, his training of his horse, and his playing with his wife, for these are from the truth. And whoever learns archery and then abandons it, it is a blessing (نعمة) that he has been ungrateful for."

I say: There are other narrations with these meanings, especially regarding horses and archery. In any case, the narrations are by way of enumerating instances (المصاديق).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa'd, al-Harith ibn Abi Usamah, Abu Ya'la, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Qani' in his Mu'jam, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mandah, al-Ruyani in his Musnad, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir extracted from Yazid ibn Abdullah ibn Gharib from his father from his grandfather from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: وَآخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ اللهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ "...and others besides them, whom you do not know [but] whom Allah knows" [Qarai 8:60]—he said: "They are the Jinn; and Satan does not afflict a human with madness in a house where there is a noble horse."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning. The gist of these narrations is to link the saying: وَآخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ اللهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ with the saying: وَمِنْ رِباطِ الْخَيْلِ "and of steeds of war." This is of the nature of application (الجري) and is not part of the formal exegesis (التفسير) at all. The verse, in its apparent meaning (بظاهرها), refers to human enemies such as the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abza that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to recite: وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ (And if they incline toward peace).

In the same source, Abu Ubayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها "And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it..." [Qarai 8:61]—he said: "This verse was abrogated by the verse: قاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَلا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ 'Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day...' until His saying: صاغِرُونَ '...while they are humbled' [Qarai 9:29]."

I say: Its abrogation was also narrated by the verse of Bara'ah: فاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ "kill the polytheists wherever you find them" [Qarai 9:5]. However, the verse is not devoid of an allusion to the ruling being temporary, as He said: وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ "And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it, and put your trust in Allah. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" [Qarai 8:61].

In al-Kafi, with its chain from al-Halabi from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها—I asked: "What is peace (السلم)?" He said: "Entering into our matter." In another narration: "Entering into your matter."

I say: This is of the nature of application (الجري).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Asakir extracted from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "It is written on the Throne: 'There is no god but I alone, I have no partner; Muhammad is My servant and My Messenger, I aided him through Ali.' And that is His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِ وَبِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'It is He who strengthened you with His help and with the faithful' [Qarai 8:62]."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Ma'ani with his chain from Abu Hurayrah, and Abu Nu'aym in Hilyat al-Awliya' with his chain from him, as did Ibn Shahrashub with a chain from Anas from the Prophet (ص).

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Sharaf al-Din al-Najafi, who said: "Its interpretation (تأويله) was mentioned by Abu Nu'aym in Hilyat al-Awliya' through his chain from Abu Hurayrah, who said: 'This verse was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib, and he is the one intended by His saying: المؤمنين (the faithful).'"

I say: The wording of the verse does not readily support this, unless what is intended by "following" is the complete following from which no aspect of one's affairs deviates, and the word "from" (من) is for partitivity (التبعيض) rather than explanation (البيان), if the context (السياق) allows for it.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bazzar extracted from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When Umar embraced Islam, the polytheists said: 'The people have gained half of us today,' and Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ ـ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'O Prophet! Allah is sufficient for you and those of the faithful who follow you' [Qarai 8:64]."

I say: This meaning was narrated in other reports, but reason (الاعتبار) does not support it. The time in which he embraced Islam was not of a nature that would validate an address such as: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ; those were days of trial and hardship, and that state continued for many long years. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at that time did not need anything to aid him in terms of numbers. In these narrations, it is said that he was the completion of forty or the forty-fourth. Furthermore, it is apparent that the verse is Madinan, being among the verses of Surah al-Anfal.

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from al-Zuhri regarding His saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ—he said: "It was revealed concerning the Ansar."

I say: The context (سياق) of the verse does not support this, just as it did not support the previous two narrations, unless what is intended is its revelation on the day the Ansar believed in him or the day they followed him. It appears that the verse was revealed to comfort the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with all those who were with him of the believers: both the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار). It serves as a preamble and preparation for what follows in the next verse regarding the command to urge the believers to fight.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: "He said: 'The ruling at the beginning of the Prophethood regarding the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was that a single man was obligated to fight ten of the disbelievers; if he fled from them, he was one who fled from the battlefield (الفار من الزحف), and a hundred would fight a thousand.'"

"Then Allah knew that there was weakness in them and they were unable to do that, so Allah revealed: الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً ـ فَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ صابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ 'Now Allah has lightened it for you, and He knew that there is a weakness in you. So if there be a hundred of you, patient and steadfast, they shall overcome two hundred...' [Qarai 8:66]. Thus, He made it obligatory for the least of the believers to fight two of the disbelievers; if he flees from them, he is one who flees from the battlefield. But if there are three disbelievers against one Muslim and the Muslim flees from them, he is not one who flees from the battlefield."

I say: In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Husayn ibn Salih from al-Sadiq from Ali (عليه‌السلام), there is something close to this. What is in its meaning was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through numerous chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Shirazi in al-Alqab, Ibn Adi, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) extracted from Ibn Umar: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ ـ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً with the word 'weakness' (ضعفاً) pronounced with a damma (رفع) [on the dad]."

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِنَبِىٍّ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَسْرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يُثْخِنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٧)
A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly decimated [the enemy] in the land. You desire the transitory gains of this world, while Allah desires [for you] [the reward of] the Hereafter, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (67)
لَّوْلَا كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيمَآ أَخَذْتُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٦٨)
Had it not been for a prior decree of Allah, surely there would have befallen you a great punishment for what you took. (68)
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٩)
Avail yourselves of the spoils you have taken as lawful and good, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (69)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّمَن فِىٓ أَيْدِيكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَسْرَىٰٓ إِن يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْرًا يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْرًا مِّمَّآ أُخِذَ مِنكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧٠)
O Prophet! Say to the captives who are in your hands, ‘If Allah finds any good in your hearts, He will give you [something which is] better than what has been taken away from you, and He will forgive you, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (70)
وَإِن يُرِيدُوا۟ خِيَانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خَانُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٧١)
But if they seek to betray you, then they have already betrayed Allah earlier, and He gave [you] power over them; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (71)

(Commentary)

This is a rebuke from Allah, the Glorified, to the people of Badr when they took captives from among the polytheists (المشركين) and then suggested to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he should not kill them, but rather take ransom (الفداء) from them to improve their own condition and strengthen themselves against the enemies of the religion (الدين). Allah, the Glorified, was severe in this rebuke, yet He granted their request and permitted them to utilize the spoils of war (الغنائم), which included the ransom.

At the end of the verses, there is a form of encouragement and a beautiful promise for the captives if they embrace Islam, and a declaration of being independent of them should they intend to betray the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — It is not for a prophet to take captives until he has fully established his hold on the land. (8:67) until the end of the three verses. Captivity (الأسر): It is the binding of a combatant such that he falls into the grasp of the one taking him, as has been said. The captive (الأسير) is the one who is bound; its plural forms are asrā (الأسرى), asara’ (الأسراء), asārā (الأسارى), and usārā (الأسارى). It is said that asārā is a plural of a plural. Based on this, taking prisoners (السبي) is broader in application than captivity (الأسر), as it applies to taking those who do not require binding, such as women and children (الذراري).

Thikhan (الثخن), with a kasrah then a fathah, means thickness or density (الغلظ). From this comes the expression: "Wounds have weakened him (أثخنته الجراح)" and "Illness has overcome him (أثخنه المرض)." Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "It is said: a thing is thakhuna (ثخن), so it is thakhīn (ثخين), when it becomes thick such that it does not flow or continue its movement. From this is borrowed the expression: 'I overcame him (أثخنته) with striking and humiliation.'" Allah, the Exalted, said: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — It is not for a prophet to take captives until he has fully established his hold on the land (8:67), and: حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ — until you have subdued them, then take [them] as captives (47:4). Thus, the meaning of the Prophet's "establishing hold (إثخان) in the land" is the stabilization of his religion among the people, as if it were something thick that has solidified and become firm after it was thin and fluid, feared to vanish by flowing away.

Transitory gain (العرض) is that which happens to a thing and is quick to vanish. For this reason, the goods of this world are called ‘arad (عرض) due to their perishability and imminent disappearance. Lawful (الحلال) is a description derived from al-hill (الحل), the opposite of a knot (العقد) or prohibition (الحرمة); it is as if the lawful thing was tied up and forbidden, then untied afterward. The meaning of wholesome (الطيب) has already been mentioned: that which is agreeable to one's nature (الطبع).

The commentators have differed in the interpretation of these verses after agreeing that they were revealed after the Battle of Badr to rebuke the people of Badr and permit the spoils for them. The reason for the difference lies in the various narrations (الأخبار) regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) and the meanings of its phrases. If the narrations were authentic, reflection upon them would indicate an extraordinary latitude in transmitting hadith by meaning (الحديث بالمعنى), to the point that the narrations sometimes differ like contradictory reports.

Consequently, the interpretations varied according to these differences. Some suggest that the rebuke and threat are directed at the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and all the believers; or to the Prophet and the believers except for Umar; or except for Umar and Sa'd ibn Mu'adh; or to the believers excluding the Prophet; or to a specific person or persons who suggested the ransom after he consulted them.

Some say the rebuke is for taking the ransom, or for deeming the spoils lawful before permission was granted by Allah, and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) shared in this because he initiated the consultation with them—even though the people only took the ransom after the revelation of the verses, not before, such that they would be rebuked for it. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is far too exalted for it to be permissible in his right to deem something lawful before Allah permits it for him and reveals it to him. Far be it from the presence of the Truth, the Glorified, to threaten His Prophet with a "great punishment" which is not befitting to descend upon him without a sin he committed, while He has protected him from sins (المعاصي). A "great punishment" only descends for a great sin, not as some have said that it refers to minor sins (الصغائر).

What ought to be said is that His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — It is not for a prophet to take captives until he has fully established his hold on the land (8:67) means that the established sunnah (السنة) among the past prophets (عليه‌السلام) was that when they fought their enemies and triumphed over them, they would make an example of them by killing, so that those behind them would take heed and desist from opposing Allah and His Messenger. They would not take captives until they had established a firm hold (يثخنوا) in the land and their religion was stabilized among the people. After that, there is no barrier to taking captives, followed by either grace (المن) or ransom (الفداء), as Allah said in what He revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after the matter of Islam became dominant and established in the Hijaz and Yemen: فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِداءً — So when you confront the faithless, strike their necks. When you have subdued them, then take [them] as captives, and afterwards either set them free out of grace or for a ransom (47:4).

The rebuke, as indicated by the context of the speech in the first verse, is for their taking of captives, as evidenced also by His saying in the second verse: لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَخَذْتُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ — there would have surely befallen you a great punishment for what you took (8:68), meaning "in your taking." At the time the verses were revealed, they had taken captives but not yet the ransom. Thus, the rebuke is not for deeming the ransom permissible or taking it, as has been conjectured.

Rather, His saying in the following verse: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلالاً طَيِّباً وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — Eat of what you have taken as spoil, lawful and wholesome, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful (8:69)—where it begins with the fa of result (فاء التفريع) which branches its meaning from what preceded it—testifies that "spoil (الغنيمة)" here includes the ransom. It indicates that they suggested to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not to kill the captives and to take ransom from them, just as they asked him about the "accessions (الأنفال)" or asked him to give it to them, as in the verse at the beginning of the Surah. How could it be imagined that they would ask him for the accessions but not ask him to take the ransom, when the ransom taken—according to the narrations—was nearly two hundred and eighty thousand dirhams?

Thus, they had asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to give them the spoils and to take the ransom for them. So Allah rebuked them fundamentally for taking the captives, then He permitted for them that for which they had taken the captives, which is the ransom. This was not because the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) shared with them in deeming the ransom permissible or in consulting them regarding ransom versus killing such that he would share in the rebuke directed at them.

Evidence from the wording of the verse that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) does not share in the rebuke is that the rebuke in the verse is attached to "taking captives," and there is nothing in it to suggest that he consulted them about it or was pleased with it. It has not been reported in any of the traditions (الآثار) that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) enjoined them to take captives, nor did he say anything suggesting approval of it. Rather, it was something the general body of the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار) undertook according to their custom in wars: when they triumphed over an enemy, they took captives for enslavement or ransom. It is mentioned in the traditions that they went to extremes in taking captives, and a man would protect his captive from being harmed by others—except for Ali (عليه‌السلام), who killed many men and did not take a single captive.

So the meaning of the verses is: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍ — It is not for a prophet—and it has not been known in the sunnah of Allah regarding His prophets—أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى — to take captives—and be entitled to take them and derive some benefit from that—حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ — until he has fully established his hold—and become firm—فِي الْأَرْضِ — on the land—and his religion is stabilized among the people. تُرِيدُونَ — You desire—O people of Badr (and the address is to all with this generality which includes a rebuke for all, since most of them were involved in suggesting the ransom to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله))—عَرَضَ الدُّنْيا — the transitory gain of this world—and its goods that quickly vanish—وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ — while Allah desires [for you] the Hereafter—by legislating the religion and commanding the fighting of the disbelievers, and then through this sunnah which He has informed of in His speech. وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ — And Allah is All-mighty (عزيز)—He is not overcome—حَكِيمٌ — All-wise (حكيم)—He does not act in vain in His perfected rulings.

لَوْ لا كِتابٌ مِنَ اللهِ سَبَقَ — Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah (8:68)—which necessitates that He does not punish you or destroy you. It was left vague (إبهام) because vagueness is more appropriate in a context of rebuke, so that the listener's mind may go to every possible extent, and it is not specified, which would make the matter seem light to him. لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَخَذْتُمْ — there would have surely befallen you for what you took—meaning in your taking of captives, for the ransom and the spoils were not taken before the revelation of the verses and the notification of their lawfulness and wholesomeness—عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ — a great punishment. This, as mentioned, indicates the greatness of the sin (المعصية), because a great punishment is only deserved for a great sin. فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ — Eat of what you have taken as spoil (8:69)—and utilize the benefit you have secured, whether it be from the wealth of the polytheists you gained power over or the ransom you took from them—حَلالاً طَيِّباً — lawful and wholesome—that is, in the state of being lawful and wholesome by the permission of Allah, the Glorified. وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. This is a justification (تعليل) for His saying: "Eat of what you have taken as spoil," etc., meaning: We have forgiven you and had mercy on you, so eat of what you have taken as spoil. Or it is a justification for all that preceded, meaning: Allah did not punish you, but rather permitted it for you because He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ مِنَ الْأَسْرى — O Prophet! Say to the captives who are in your hands (8:70). The captives being "in their hands" is a metaphor (استعارة) for their complete dominance over them, like a thing in a person's hand that he turns over however he wishes.

His saying: إِنْ يَعْلَمِ اللهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْراً — ‘If Allah knows any good in your hearts—is an allusion (كناية) to faith (الإيمان) or following the truth which faith necessitates. For He, the Exalted, promises them forgiveness at the end of the verse, and there is no forgiveness with polytheism (شرك). He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes (4:48).

The meaning of the verse is: O Prophet, say to those captives in your hands over whom you have gained dominance and from whom you have taken ransom: if faith is established in your hearts and Allah knows that of you—and He only knows that which is established and realized—He will give you better than the ransom that was taken from you and will forgive you, and Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ يُرِيدُوا خِيانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خانُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ — But if they seek to betray you, they have already betrayed Allah before, and He gave [you] mastery over them (8:71). "He gave mastery over them (أمكنه منه)" means He gave him power over them. He said first "your betrayal (خيانتك)" and then said "they betrayed Allah (خانوا الله)" because they intended by the ransom to regroup and return to fighting him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). As for their betrayal of Allah before, it was their disbelief (كفر) and their persistence in trying to extinguish the light of Allah and their plotting and deception.

The meaning of the verse is: If they believe in Allah and faith is established in their hearts, Allah will give them better than what was taken from them and forgive them. But if they intend to betray you and return to their previous state of stubbornness and corruption, they have already betrayed Allah before, so He gave you mastery over them and empowered you against them, and He is capable of doing that to them again. Allah is All-Knowing (عليم) of their betrayal if they were to betray, All-Wise (حكيم) in giving you mastery over them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى — It is not for a prophet to take captives (8:67) etc.—he said: The slain among the polytheists on the day of Badr were seventy—Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام) killed twenty-seven of them (1), and the captives were also seventy. None of the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were taken captive. They gathered the captives, bound them in ropes, and drove them on foot. Nine men from the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were killed—among them was Sa'd ibn Khaythama, who was one of the leaders (النقباء) of the Aws.

He said: Narrated from Muhammad ibn Ishaq: Eleven men from the Muslims were martyred on the day of Badr: four from Quraysh and seven from the Helpers (الأنصار); it is also said eight. Over forty men from the polytheists were killed (2).

He said: Narrated from Ibn Abbas: When evening came for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the day of Badr—and the people were held in bonds—he stayed awake the first part of the night. His companions said to him: "What is the matter with you that you do not sleep?" He (عليه‌السلام) said: "I heard the moaning of my uncle al-Abbas in his bonds." So they released him, and he became silent, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) slept.

He said: Ubaydah al-Salmani narrated from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said to his companions on the day of Badr regarding the captives: "If you wish, you may kill them, and if you wish, you may take ransom for them—and an equal number of you shall be martyred [later]." There were seventy captives. They said: "Rather, we will take the ransom and enjoy it—and strengthen ourselves against our enemy, and let an equal number of us be martyred." Ubaydah said: "They sought both of the two good options (3)," so seventy of them were killed on the day of Uhud.

In the book of Ali ibn Ibrahim: When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) killed al-Nadr ibn al-Harith and Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt—the Helpers feared he would kill the captives—so they said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have killed seventy—and they are your people and your kinsmen; will you find their root? So take from them, O Messenger of Allah, the ransom." They had already taken what they found of the spoils in the camp of Quraysh. When they requested this of him and asked him, the verse was revealed: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى — It is not for a prophet to take captives (8:67)—so he permitted that for them.

The highest ransom was four thousand dirhams—and the lowest was one thousand dirhams. Quraysh sent the ransom one by one. Zaynab, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), sent the ransom for her husband, Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi‘; she sent necklaces of hers that Khadija had prepared for her. Abu al-As was the son of Khadija's sister. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw those necklaces, he said: "May Allah have mercy on Khadija; these are necklaces she prepared for her." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) released him on the condition that he send Zaynab to him and not prevent her from joining him. He made a covenant with him for that and fulfilled it.

He said: It is narrated that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) disliked taking the ransom until he saw Sa'd ibn Mu'adh—dislike for it was on his face—so he said: "O Messenger of Allah, this is the first war in which we have met the group of polytheists—and establishing a firm hold (الإثخان) by killing is more beloved to me than keeping the men alive." Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "O Messenger of Allah, they called you a liar and expelled you—so bring them forward and strike their necks; and empower Ali over Aqil so he may strike his neck, and empower me over so-and-so so I may strike his neck, for these are the leaders of disbelief (أئمة الكفر)." Abu Bakr said: "Your family and your people; be gentle with them and keep them alive—and take from them a ransom so it may be a strength for us against the disbelievers." Ibn Zayd said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "If a punishment were to descend from the sky, none of you would be saved—except for Umar and Sa'd ibn Mu'adh."

Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: The ransom on the day of Badr for every man of the polytheists was forty uqiyahs, and an uqiyah is forty mithqals—except for al-Abbas, for his ransom was one hundred uqiyahs. Twenty uqiyahs of gold had been taken from him when he was captured. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "That is spoil (غنيمة)—so ransom yourself and your two nephews, Nawfal and Aqil." He said: "I have nothing with me." He said: "Where is the gold that you handed over to Umm al-Fadl and said: 'If something happens to me, it is for you, and for al-Fadl, Abdullah, Qutham'?" He said: "Who informed you of this?" He said: "Allah, the Exalted." He said: "I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah—by Allah, no one was aware of this except Allah, the Exalted."

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous from the paths of both parties (الفريقين); we have omitted them for the sake of brevity.

In Qurb al-Isnad by al-Himyari, from Abdullah ibn Maymun, from Ja'far, from his father (عليه‌السلام), he said: Some wealth in dirhams was brought to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to al-Abbas: "O Abbas, spread out a cloak and take a portion of this wealth." So he spread out a cloak and took a group of it. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abbas, this is from that which Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ مِنَ الْأَسْرى ـ إِنْ يَعْلَمِ اللهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْراً يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْراً مِمَّا أُخِذَ مِنْكُمْ — O Prophet! Say to the captives who are in your hands: ‘If Allah knows any good in your hearts, He will give you [something] better than what has been taken from you (8:70).'" He said: It was revealed regarding al-Abbas, Nawfal, and Aqil.

And he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade on the day of Badr that anyone from the Banu Hashim or Abu al-Bakhtari be killed. They were taken captive, so he sent Ali and said: "Look who is here from the Banu Hashim." He said: He passed by Aqil ibn Abi Talib and turned away from him. He said: So he said to him: "O son of Ali's mother, by Allah, you have indeed seen my place."

He said: So he returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "This is Abu al-Fadl in the hand of so-and-so, and this is Aqil in the hand of so-and-so, and this is Nawfal in the hand of so-and-so"—meaning Nawfal ibn al-Harith. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went until he reached Aqil and said: "O Abu Yazid, Abu Jahl has been killed!" He said: "Then do not dispute over Tihama." He said: "If you have established a firm hold (أثخنتم) over the people, [well and good]; otherwise, ride their shoulders."

He said: So al-Abbas was brought, and it was said to him: "Ransom yourself and ransom your two nephews." He said: "O Muhammad, will you leave me to beg Quraysh with my palm?" So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: "Give from that which you left behind with Umm al-Fadl and said to her: 'If something happens to me in my journey, then spend it on your children and yourself.'" He said: "O son of my brother, who informed you of this?" He said: "Gabriel brought it to me." He said: "By an oath, no one knew of this except me and her—I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah." He said: "All the captives returned as polytheists—except for al-Abbas, Aqil, and Nawfal ibn al-Harith." And regarding them was revealed this verse: قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ مِنَ الْأَسْرى — Say to the captives who are in your hands (8:70).

I say: These meanings are narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various paths from the companions. The revelation of the verse regarding al-Abbas and his two nephews is narrated from Ibn Sa'd and Ibn Asakir from Ibn Abbas. The amount of the ransom paid for each man of the captives and the story of al-Abbas's ransom for himself and his two nephews is narrated by al-Tabarsi in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan from al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) as in the hadith.

(1) And he did not take anyone captive according to the narrations. (2) These are the ones whose names were recorded by the scholars of history, besides those whose names were not recorded. (3) But His saying, the Exalted, in rebuking them: "You desire the transitory gain of this world," proves Ubaydah wrong in his statement.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ مَا لَكُم مِّن وَلَـٰيَتِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنِ ٱسْتَنصَرُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٧٢)
Indeed those who have believed and migrated and waged jihad with their possessions and persons in the way of Allah, and those who gave [them] shelter and help—they are heirs of one another. As for those who have believed but did not migrate, you have no heirdom in relation to them whatsoever until they migrate. Yet if they ask your help for the sake of religion, it is incumbent on you to help them, excepting against a people with whom you have a treaty; and Allah watches what you do. (72)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتْنَةٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَفَسَادٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧٣)
As for the faithless, they are heirs of one another. Unless you do the same, there will be strife in the land and great corruption. (73)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٧٤)
Those who have believed, migrated, and waged jihad in the way of Allah, and those who gave them shelter and help, it is they who are truly the faithful. For them shall be forgiveness and a noble provision. (74)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ مَعَكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (٧٥)
Those who believed afterwards and migrated, and waged jihad along with you, they belong to you; but the blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (75)

(Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah, and their meaning returns in a sense to what the Surah opened with. They contain the obligation of mutual alliance (الموالاة) — the bond of loyalty, protection, and support — among the believers, except when they differ in migration (المهاجرة) or the lack thereof, and the severance of alliance with the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهاجَرُوا وَجاهَدُوا (Indeed those who believed and migrated and waged jihad...) until His saying: أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ (...are allies of one another). What is meant by "those who believed and migrated" is the first group of the Migrants (المهاجرون) before the revelation of this Surah, as evidenced by the mention of later migrants at the end of the verses. What is meant by "those who gave shelter and helped" are the Helpers (الأنصار) — the residents of Medina who welcomed and supported the Prophet and the Migrants — who gave shelter to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believing Migrants and helped Allah and His Messenger. At that time, the Muslims were confined to these two groups, except for a few who had believed in Mecca but had not migrated.

Allah established an alliance (ولاية) between them by His saying: أُولئِكَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ (they are allies of one another). This alliance (الولاية) is broader than the alliance of inheritance (ولاية الميراث), the alliance of support (ولاية النصرة), or the alliance of security (ولاية الأمن). Thus, if any of them granted asylum to a disbeliever, it was binding upon all; some of the whole were the allies of the rest of the whole. A Migrant was an ally to every Migrant and Helper, and a Helper was an ally to every Helper and Migrant. All of this is evidenced by the absolute (إطلاق) use of the term "alliance" (الولاية) in the verse.

Therefore, there is no evidence to restrict the verse to the alliance of inheritance through the brotherhood (المواخاة) that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله established at the beginning of the Migration (الهجرة) between the Migrants and the Helpers, by which they used to inherit from one another for a time until it was abrogated (نسخت).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يُهاجِرُوا (And those who believed but did not migrate...) to the end of the verse. Its meaning is clear; it negates the alliance (الولاية) between the believing Migrants and Helpers on one hand, and the non-migrating believers on the other, except for the alliance of support (ولاية النصرة) if they seek help from them, provided that the seeking of help is not against a people with whom the believers have a treaty (ميثاق).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ (As for the faithless, they are allies of one another). That is, their alliance is among themselves and does not extend beyond them to the believers; thus, it is not for the believers to take them as allies. This is because His saying here regarding the disbelievers, "they are allies of one another," is like His saying regarding the believers, "they are allies of one another"—it is an enactment and legislation (إنشاء وتشريع) presented in the form of a report (إخبار). Establishing the alliance among the disbelievers themselves cannot be understood, rationally speaking, except as we have mentioned: the negation of its extension from them to the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُنْ فِتْنَةٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَفَسادٌ كَبِيرٌ (Unless you do it, there will be conduct of discord in the earth and a great corruption). This is an allusion to the wisdom (مصلحة) behind establishing the alliance in the manner it was established. Alliance (الولاية) is something indispensable in any human society, especially the Islamic society which is founded upon following the truth and spreading divine justice. Conversely, taking the disbelievers as allies—when they are the enemies of this society—leads to intermingling with them, whereby their creeds and morals permeate the community. Thus, the Islamic way of life (سيرة), built upon the truth, would be corrupted by their ways, which are built upon following whims (اتباع الهوى) and the worship of Satan. The course of events in recent times has confirmed what this verse alluded to.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهاجَرُوا (And those who believed and migrated...) to the end of the verse. This is an affirmation of the right of faith for those who have truly characterized themselves with its effects, and a promise to them of forgiveness and a noble provision (الرزق الكريم).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَهاجَرُوا وَجاهَدُوا مَعَكُمْ فَأُولئِكَ مِنْكُمْ (And those who believed afterwards and migrated and waged jihad with you, they are of you). This is an address to the first Migrants and the Helpers, joining those who believed, migrated, and waged jihad with them to their rank, so that they share with them in the alliance (الولاية).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ (And the blood relatives are more entitled to one another in the Book of Allah...) to the end of the verse. This establishes the alliance (الولاية) among blood relatives (أولو الأرحام) and kin, which is the alliance of inheritance (ولاية الإرث), as the other types of alliance are not restricted to them.

The verse abrogates (تنسخ) the alliance of inheritance through brotherhood (المواخاة) that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله implemented among the Muslims at the start of the Migration. It establishes inheritance through kinship (القرابة), whether there is a fixed-share heir (ذو سهم) or not, or whether there is a residuary heir (عصبة) or not. The verse is absolute (مطلقة), as is apparent.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: "They used to inherit from one another through brotherhood (المواخاة)."

I say: There is no indication in this that the verse was revealed specifically regarding the alliance of brothers.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام, who said: "The maternal uncle and maternal aunt inherit if there is no one else with them—for Allah says: وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ ـ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ (And the blood relatives are more entitled to one another in the Book of Allah)."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi from Abu Basir from him in a disconnected (مرسلا) form.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah: وَأُولُوا الْأرحامِ بَعضُهُم أَولى بِبَعضٍ في كِتابِ اللهِ (And the blood relatives are more entitled to one another in the Book of Allah): "Indeed, some of them are more entitled to the inheritance than others—because the closest of them to him is more entitled to him." Then Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام said: "They are more entitled to the deceased, and the closest of them to him are his mother, his brother, and his sister from his mother and father—is the mother not closer to the deceased than his brothers and sisters?"

In the same source, from Ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "When Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام and Uthman ibn Affan differed regarding a man who dies leaving no residuary heirs (عصبة) to inherit from him, but having relatives (ذوو قرابة) who do not [normally] inherit from him because he has no fixed share (مفروض) among them. Ali عليه‌السلام said: 'His inheritance belongs to his relatives—because Allah the Exalted says: وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ (And the blood relatives are more entitled to one another in the Book of Allah).' Uthman said: 'I shall place his inheritance in the public treasury (بيت مال) of the Muslims—and none of his relatives shall inherit from him.'"

I say: The narrations negating the doctrine of residuary priority (العصبة) and relying upon this verse for that purpose are numerous from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tayalisi, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله established brotherhood between his companions, and they inherited from one another until this verse was revealed: وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ (And the blood relatives are more entitled to one another in the Book of Allah). So they abandoned that and inherited through lineage (النسب)."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission reaching Musa ibn Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding what transpired between him and Harun [al-Rashid]—and in it: "Harun said: 'Why then did you claim that you inherited from the Messenger of Allah, while the paternal uncle (العم) excludes the son of the paternal uncle (ابن العم)? The Messenger of Allah passed away while Abu Talib had died before him, and al-Abbas, his paternal uncle, was alive...'" until he said: "I said: 'Indeed, the Prophet did not grant inheritance to one who did not migrate, nor did he establish an alliance (ولاية) for him until he migrated.' He said: 'What is your proof for that?' I said: 'The saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يُهاجِرُوا ـ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ وَلايَتِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ حَتَّى يُهاجِرُوا (As for those who believed but did not migrate, you have no alliance with them until they migrate). And my uncle al-Abbas did not migrate.' He said: 'I ask you, O Musa—have you given this legal opinion (أفتيت) to any of our enemies, or informed any of the jurists (الفقهاء) regarding this matter?' I said: 'By Allah, no; and no one has asked me about it except the Commander of the Faithful (امير المؤمنين).'" — the tradition.

I say: It was also narrated by al-Mufid in al-Ikhtisas.
